coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric-SERS dual-mode sensing of Pb(II) ions in traditional Chinese medicine samples based on carbon dots-capped gold nanoparticles as nanozyme
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yifan
Li, Qiulan
Yang, Dezhi
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Peroxidase (POD)-mimicking nanozymes have got great progress in the sensing field, but most nanozyme assaying systems are built with a single-signal output mode, which is vulnerable to the effect of different factors. Thus, establishment of a dual-signal output mode is necessary for acquiring dependable and durable performance. This work described an Fe doped noradrenaline-based carbon dots and Prussian blue (Fe,NA-CDs/PB) nanocomposite as a POD-like nanozyme and modified gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the colorimetric and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) dual-mode sensor of Pb(II) in traditional Chinese medicine samples. With 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the substrates, it was found that the addition of Pb(II) inhibited the POD-like activity of Fe,NA-CDs/PB and AuNPs, so it was used for colorimetric and SERS dual-mode assays. The POD-like activity was shown to be a “ping-pong” catalytic mechanism, whereas the addition of Pb(II) produced noncompetitive inhibition with modulatory effects on Fe,NA-CDs/PB. The linear response range for colorimetric and SERS sensor detection of Pb(II) was 0.01–1.00 mg/L with the detection limit of 5 μg/L and 8 μg/L, respectively. This dual-mode detection system shows excellent selectivity. More importantly, the Pb(II) in traditional Chinese medicine samples have successfully assayed with good recovery from 90.4 to 108.9 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric/SERS dual-mode probe
SERS-active
Peroxidase-like Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticle

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Fe,NA-CDs/PB carbon quantum dots
Colorimetric and SERS detection of Pb(II)
Preparation of herbal medicines prior to Pb(II) analysis
Sensitive detection of Pb(II) based on UV and SERS
Specificity of the proposed colorimetric sensor and SERS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was greatly supported by the Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117) and Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 202M20212118105).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Fe,NA-CDs/PB and Fe,NA-CDs/PB@AuNPs. (A)TEM image of Fe,NA-CDs/PB. (B) HRTEM image of Fe,NA-CDs/PB. (C) TEM image of Fe,NA-CDs/PB@AuNPs. (D) HRTEM image of Fe,NA-CDs/PB@AuNPs. (E-H) XPS spectra. (I) XRD spectra. (J-K) FTIR spectra. (L) UV–Vis spectra of the Fe,NA-CDs/PB@AuNPs and Na3CA@AuNPs. (M) SERS spectra of TMB, TMB + H2O2, AuNPs + TMB + H2O2, AuNPs + TMB + H2O2 + Pb(II).
(A-D) Steady state kinetic assays. The corresponding double reciprocal (Lineweaver-Burk) equation of Fe,NA-CDs/PB.
(A) Effect of pH. (B) Effect of reaction times. (C) Effect of temperature. (D) Effect of Fe,NA-CDs/PB concentration.

(A) UV–vis spectra for Fe,NA-CDs/PB-ABTS system with the different concentration of Pb(II) from 0.01 to 1.00 mg/L at 415 and 736 nm. (B) linear curve of characteristic peak intensity and Pb(II) concentration. (C) SERS spectra of AuNPs - TMB with different Pb(II) content (0.01–1.00 mg/L). (D) Corresponding linear equation of the SERS intensity at 1607 cm−1 with the different content of Pb(II). (E-F) SERS signals of Blank and two concentrations of Pb(II) at the same laser fluence (n = 3).
(A) Selectivity of UV–vis spectra for Fe,NA-CDs/PB-ABTS system. (B) Selectivity of SERS spectra of AuNPs-TMB system for Pb(II) detection.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of the UV-SERS dual-mode sensor for detection of Pb(II) content.
Determination of Pb(II) content in actual samples by UV–vis spectra (n = 3).
Determination of Pb(II) content in actual samples by SERS spectra (n = 3).
Comparison of the analytical performance of the proposed the colorimetric-SERS dual-mode Pb(II) sensor with others.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric-SERS dual-mode sensing of Pb(II) ions in traditional Chinese medicine samples based on carbon dots-capped gold nanoparticles as nanozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Peroxidase (POD)-mimicking nanozymes have got great progress in the sensing field, but most nanozyme assaying systems are built with a single-signal output mode, which is vulnerable to the effect of different factors. Thus, establishment of a dual-signal output mode is necessary for acquiring dependable and durable performance. This work described an Fe doped noradrenaline-based carbon dots and Prussian blue (Fe,NA-CDs/PB) nanocomposite as a POD-like nanozyme and modified gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the colorimetric and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) dual-mode sensor of Pb(II) in traditional Chinese medicine samples. With 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the substrates, it was found that the addition of Pb(II) inhibited the POD-like activity of Fe,NA-CDs/PB and AuNPs, so it was used for colorimetric and SERS dual-mode assays. The POD-like activity was shown to be a “ping-pong” catalytic mechanism, whereas the addition of Pb(II) produced noncompetitive inhibition with modulatory effects on Fe,NA-CDs/PB. The linear response range for colorimetric and SERS sensor detection of Pb(II) was 0.01–1.00 mg/L with the detection limit of 5 μg/L and 8 μg/L, respectively. This dual-mode detection system shows excellent selectivity. More importantly, the Pb(II) in traditional Chinese medicine samples have successfully assayed with good recovery from 90.4 to 108.9 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a small-sized carbon material with a material size of less than 10 nm [1], and due to their unique optical properties, easy surface modification and low toxicity, have been widely studied and applied in the fields of sensing and biology [2]. And CDs with enzyme-like properties to catalyze the reaction have been found [3–6]. The CDs with heteroatom doping and metal could not only increase the number of catalytically active centers but also affect the charge allocation [7–9]. It was confirmed in previous studies that the active sites were increased by doping Fe CDs throughout the POD-like catalytic process, exhibiting higher catalytic activity [10–14].

The Prussian blue nanoparticles (PB NPs), as a kind of iron-based nanomaterial, has been proven to own higher POD-like activity than Fe3O4 NPs [15,16]. Thus, some PB-based nanocomposites were explored for the colorimetric determination of various analytes [17]. However, the blue color and poor dispersion of PB NPs in aqueous solution limits its application in the colorimetric sensor. To overcome this limit, PB NPs provide synergistic catalytic effects by combining with other materials, improving dispersibility and reducing interference [18–20].

Pb(II), as a common heavy metal pollutant, has a chronic accumulation [21,22]. Heavy metal contamination in traditional Chinese medicines (TCM) is the main element in examining the safety of TCM and has become the key to quality control of TCM. The control of heavy metals in Chinese medicines has been restricted in the pharmacopoeias of many countries, such as the 2020 edition of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia [23,24]. Pb(II) is considered to be the most representative heavy metal element in the quality control of TCM [25]. Therefore, it is essential to develop new analytical approaches for rapid and sensitive Pb(II) detection in TCM samples [26].

So far, a variety of analytical techniques for Pb(II) detection have been developed, including ion chromatography (IC), fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and sensor approach etc [27–29]. Compared to other methods, colorimetric and SERS sensor methods are often considered a potential rapid and specific assay. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), as a type of effect analysis means with rapid detection, specificity, and high sensitivity, has high sensitivity for trace detection at the single molecule level, ions, etc [30–34]. However, direct testing of the molecule on the SERS substrate is challenging since a low intensity of the Raman scattered signal [35–37]. To address this constraint, some new SERS sensor of combining nanozyme was researched. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), one of the most widely studied nanoparticles with intrinsic multifunctionality such as SERS properties and enzyme-like activities, has been regarded as the promising nanozymes for SERS analysis [38,39]. However, the low catalytic activity and poor stability of AuNPs limited their application, and more efforts are needed to improve enzyme-like activity [40,41]. Several groups have demonstrated that the CDs are beneficial for improving the catalytic activity and SERS signals of AuNPs due to ultra-thin layer structures, tiny lateral dimensions, and abundant surface functional [42–44]. And our research shows CDs-based AuNPs with SERS and POD-like activities [45–48].

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate hydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), iron nitrate hexahydrate (Fe(NO3)3⋅6H2O, 99.5 %), Cd(II), Cr(III), Co(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), Ni(II), Cu(II) and Zn(II) standards were purchased from Macklin Company (Shanghai, China). Prussian blue (PB), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS), 1,2-ethyl-enediamine (EDA, >99 %), lead standard solution (1000 mg/mL) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Noradrenaline (NA) (purity ≥ 99.0 %) and sodium citrate dihydrate (CA, C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were obtained from Xi Long Scientific Company (Shantou, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and fresh ultrapure water was used.

Fe,NA-CDs was synthesized in microwave digestion system (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German). SERS spectrum was measured using an SSR-3000 Raman spectrometer (Jianzhi Instrument Equipment Co., Ltd., China) with a 785 nm laser source. A 1910 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used to survey the absorbance. The images of transmission electron microscopy were obtained by FEI Tecnai F20 Transmission electron microscopy (FEI, American). X-ray spectra were obtained by Thermo ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Technology, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe,NA-CDs/PB carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe,NA-CDs was synthesized through a one-step microwave digestion method using a microwave digestion instrument (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, Germany). The steps were as follows: firstly, 0.1585 g of FeCl3, 0.0276 g of NA and 2.1 g of CA were added to 30 mL of deionized water, and magnetic stirring was carried out until dissolved, and then 50 µL of EDA was added to the above solution and mixed well. The previously obtained mixture was placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene tank and subjected to microwave ablation under 850 W microwave radiation, according to a set program at a temperature of 180°C for 60 min. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.25 μm membrane. The obtained filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4°C for backup. To improve POD-like activity, the synthesized CDs and PB were sonicated in a certain ratio for 6 h to obtain self-assembly Fe,NA-CDs/PB.

To maintain the successful preparation of AuNPs, all glassware used in the synthesis process was washed by thorough immersion in aqua regia (HNO3/HCl, 1:3, v/v), followed by rinsing with ultrapure water. The specific process of gold nanoparticles synthesis is as follows: 200 μL HAuCl4 (1 %) solution and 40 μL Fe,NA-CDs/PB was added into a 100 mL beaker with 20 mL purified water, heated to 90°C and stirred magnetically for 25 min. With the addition of Fe,NA-CDs/PB, the solution changed from light yellow to light pink. After completion of the color change, the synthesized AuNPs was stored at 4°C for subsequent experiments.

The absorbance values at 654 nm were determined at room temperature using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To evaluate the POD-like activity of Fe,NA-CDs/PB, a reaction mixture containing 50 mM H2O2, 0.061 mg/L of Fe,NA-CDs/PB, and 5 mM TMB were prepared. In order to carry out the kinetic experiments of Fe,NA-CDs/PB, TMB was used as the substrate, and different concentrations of H2O2 and TMB were added to determine the absorbance values at different times. According to the Michaelis-Menten equation, V = V max[S]/(K m + [S]), calculating the apparent kinetics parameters, where [S] is the concentration of substrate, V max is the maximum reaction velocity and K m value is the Michaelis-Menten constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and SERS detection of Pb(II)
ce:section.ce:para: In the established colorimetric sensor, a series of different concentrations of Pb(II) and 30 μL of Fe,NA-CDs/PB nanozymes were firstly added in a 15 mL tube and mixed well. Then the mixed liquid was incubated at 35°C for 5 min. After that, 150 μL of 50 mM H2O2 and 150 μL of 5 mM ABTS were added to the test tube. Finally, the absorption spectra at 200–800 nm were measured after filling the volume of the assay system to 4 mL with ultrapure water.

Pb(II) was detected on SERS spectra using previously prepared gold nanoparticles. Before surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy acquisition, the parameters need to be set as follows: a 785 nm laser light source at a 12 cm−1 spectral resolution, a 10 s accumulation time and an average spectral value of triplicates. 150 μL of Fe,NA-CDs/PB@AuNPs and various concentrations Pb(II) were incubated for 10 min. After that, 150 μL of 50 mM H2O2 and 150 μL of 5 mM TMB were added to the liquid phase bottle. Finally, the mixtures were subjected to Raman spectroscopy acquisition and intensity determination after 3 min of full reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of herbal medicines prior to Pb(II) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Traditional Chinese Medicine samples including Barley Yellow (Huang Ling), Salvia miltiorrhiza (Dan Cen), Astragali Radix (Huang Qi) and pomegranate peel were obtained from the Yunnan Kunming Chinese Herbal Pieces Company (Kunming, China). The dried TCM samples were thoroughly pulverized using a pulverizer, and the sample powder was obtained using a 60 mesh sieve. 300 mg of sample powder was mixed with 3.0 mL of HNO3 for 30 min. After that, the herbal medicine samples were processed using a microwave disintegrator (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German) with the following program settings: The first step was a gradient temperature increase to 120°C and held for 3 min, followed by digestion at 160°C for 3 min and finally 180°C for 5 min. The microwave digestion of the sample was completed, the obtained solution was evaporated to near dryness and diluted to 50 mL with ultrapure water.

The prepared Fe,NA-CDs/PB and AuNPs composites were characterized by TEM, XPS, IR and XRD. The following are the results of the specific characterization, the TEM images clearly show the better dispersion of Fe,NA-CDs/PB and colloidal AuNPs. Among them, Fig. 1
A and C show that the average particle sizes of the two nanozymes are 10 nm and 40 nm, respectively. The HRTEM image of Fe,NA-CDs/PB and AuNPs show that the lattice fringe spacing was 0.28 nm and 0.25 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 B and D). In order to better study the surface chemical composition and elemental states of AuNPs, AuNPs were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS of Fe,NA-CDs/PB@AuNPs exhibited in Fig. 1 E, the five typical characteristic peaks at 95, 285, 401, 532 and 710 eV are responding for the typical Au 4f, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p for AuNPs, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s in Fig. 1 F shows three peaks at 288.7 eV, 285.6 eV and 284.4 eV, which traced to C = O, C–C and C = C bonding to the surface of AuNPs. The O 1 s spectrum fits one peak at 531.9 eV, corresponding to the O = C-O- groups (Fig. 1 G). The high resolution XPS spectrum of Au 4f in Fig. 1 H clear displayed of the two peaks at 83.4 eV and 87.2 eV, which may be traced to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of metallic Au (0) and complete reduction of the Au precursor.

Fig. 1 I shows the XRD patterns of two nanozymes, representing Fe,NA-CDs/PB (blue pattern), and AuNPs (red pattern), respectively. The chemical composition of the obtained Fe,NA-CDs/PB and AuNPs were characterized by XRD patterns. Fig. 1 I shows the XRD patterns for Fe,NA-CDs/PB and AuNPs. The peaks located at 38.1°, 44.4°, 64.6° and 77.6° can be unambiguously assigned Au (111), Au (200), Au (220) and Au (311). It demonstrated the formation of crystalline AuNPs since those peaks generally matched with the standard Au XRD pattern with JCPDS file No. 00–004-0784 [54]. And the characteristic absorption peaks at 2θ = 31.1°, 36.6°, 43.2° and 61.8° can be attributed to Fe (220), Fe (311), Fe (400) and Fe (440), respectively. And the presence of some additional peak beams in the XRD graph of Fe,NA-CDs/PB is mainly due to the carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1 J, the IR peaks of the Fe,NA-CDs/PB at 769 cm−1, 1136 cm−1, 1447 cm−1, 1703 cm−1, 2084 cm−1, 3282 cm−1 and 3492 cm−1 indicate the presence of Fe-O, C-O-C, C-N, C = O, Fe2+–CN-Fe3+, C–H and O–H stretching vibration, respectively. The fabricated AuNPs had characteristic absorption peaks at 1207 cm−1, 1407 cm−1, 1714 cm−1, 2594 cm−1, 2981 cm−1 and 3560 cm−1, indicating the presence of C-OH, C-N, C = O, C = C = C, C–H and O–H (Fig. 1 K). As shown in Fig. 1 L, the monodispersed AuNPs exhibited a SPR signal at 540 nm. The main reason is that compared to citrate AuNPs with similar dimensions (CA-AuNPs), the refractive index of the dielectric environment around AuNPs on Fe,NA-CDs/PB coatings increases overall. The SERS fingerprint patterns of TMB, TMB + H2O2, AuNPs + TMB + H2O2, Blank + Pb(II) bands were investigated in Fig. 1 M. The addition of AuNPs when TMB was used as a substrate resulted in distinct signal peaks at 1184 cm−1, 1330 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1, which were attributed to the characteristic signal generated by oxTMB.

The reaction conditions, including pH, temperature, time, and the volume of Fe,NA-CDs/PB or AuNPs, also play a crucial role in SERS and colorimetric systems. The suggested sensor's many parameters were tuned for Pb(II) detection in order to achieve high sensitivity. Since AuNPs and Fe,NA-CDs/PB typically have considerable catalytic activity under acidic conditions, Pb(II) inhibition tests were conducted in a range of buffer solutions with pH values between 2 and 6. HAc-NaAc buffer solution was used to adjust the pH of the experimental system and Fig. 3
A clearly shows the effect of pH on the absorbance of the reaction system. For peroxidase activity, the ⋅OH produced is reduced under too acidic conditions, and H2O2 will decompose O2 under alkaline conditions, which will also decrease the system catalytic activity. pH 4 was finally selected as the optimum pH for the reaction system. Reaction time is another key factor affecting the rate. Fig. 3 B illustrates the change in absorbance during a 25 min period. After 20 min, the catalytic capacity reached the maximum value and remained almost constant. In the end, 10 min was chosen as the best response time. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the effect of temperature was further investigated, and it was found that the catalytic efficiency peaked at 30°C, after which the absorbance of the reaction system gradually decreased with the increase of temperature. Fig. 3 D shows the effect of different concentrations of Fe,NA-CDs/PB on the absorbance, 0.4 mg/mL was selected for further experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of Pb(II) based on UV and SERS
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis spectrophotometer spectra and SERS performance of Pb(II) were evaluated under the optimal conditions obtained. First, the color change and absorbance change of the colorimetric sensor for different amounts of Pb(II) were examined. As the concentration of Pb(II) increased, the sensing signal was linearly boosted, and the color of the sensing solution gradually changes from the original dark blue to a colorless state. As shown in Fig. 4
A, using UV–vis spectrophotometer, a linear relationship was established between the UV–vis spectra with an absorbance value of 415 nm and the concentration of Pb(II). The absorbance of Pb(II) showed good linearity in the linear range of 0.01–1.0 mg/L. The linear equation was A = -1.09c + 1.85 (A was absorbance at 415 nm, and c represents the Pb(II) concentration) and R2 of 0.992 ( Fig. 4 B). The LOD of the method was 5 μg/L.

By utilizing the 785 nm laser to calculate the SERS signal of Pb(II), the analytical properties of SERS were assessed. When TMB is used as the substrate, there are several characteristic Raman signal peaks, which are 872 cm−1, 1184 cm−1, 1330 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1, the signal peak at 1607 cm−1 was finally chosen versus the concentration of Pb(II) to establish a working curve as shown in Fig. 4 C. Furthermore, the results showed that, in comparison to the other SERS signal intensities, the one at 1607 cm−1 showed a stronger linear connection (R2 = 0.928) with the Pb(II) concentration ( Fig. 4 D). The linear equation was y = -87300.8c + 113951.3 (y was the intensity of SERS signal peaks, c was the concentration of Pb(II)), with a LOD of 8 μg/L. Finally, in order to determine the reproducibility of the sensor, different concentrations of Pb(II) were selected for reproducibility measurements ( Fig. 4 E-F), and the results showed the proposed method with good reproducibility. Based on all of the aforementioned findings, it is possible to build a workable and reasonably priced Pb(II) sensor for the purpose of identifying heavy metals in traditional Chinese medicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the proposed colorimetric sensor and SERS
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the specificity of the established colorimetric-SERS dual-mode sensor for Pb(II) detection. Selectivity experiments were performed on the detection system using Ni2+, K+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cu2+ and Na+ as interfering ions. As depicted in Fig. 5
A, when Pb(II) was added to the sensing solution, the change in absorbance was more significant compared to a solution with the same concentration of competing metal ions. As depicted in Fig. 5 B, the selectivity for Pb(II) detection was demonstrated by the fact that only Pb(II) significantly changed the absorbance value at 415 nm and the SERS intensity at 1607 cm−1, whereas the other metal ions showed less change to the system. All of the above experiments demonstrated the selectivity of the Pb(II) assay.

Taking into account the Pb(II) poisoning in Chinese medicine, herein Huang Ling, Dan Sheng and Huang Lian was used as real samples, and the application of the proposed colorimetric sensor and SERS for the detection of Pb(II) in Chinese medicine samples was evaluated. According to 2.6. Sample preparation method, the samples were digested and the microwave digestion procedure was shown in the Table 2 .

Afterwards, traditional Chinese medicine sample testing will be conducted, the results are shown in Table 3 and 4
. In colorimetric, the recoveries were in the range of 90.4 %-108.9 %, and the RSD (relative standard deviations) was 2.6 % to 4.7 %. In SERS detection, the recoveries were in the range of 92.47 %-115.8 %, and the RSD was 1.3 % to 4.9 %. The results of these studies showed that the quantitative analysis of Pb(II) in real samples was constructed using a very sensitive dual-mode detection method in colorimetric mode and SERS mode. As shown in Table 5 , the colorimetric-SERS dual-mode Pb(II) sensor in this research has a lower limit of detection (LOD) comparable with most other sensors. The suggested technique can be applied to create a workable and affordable Pb(II) sensor for conventional Chinese medicine.

As a summary, norepinephrine-based carbon dots doped with metal ions and Prussian blue (Fe,NA-CDs/PB) nanocomposites with high POD-like was synthesized. Afterwards, AuNPs was synthesized as nano-sensors using the reducing properties of carbon dots, showing good SERS activity. Based on the high POD-like activity of CDs and the excellent SERS activity of AuNPs, ABTS and TMB were used as substrates to establish colorimetric and surface-enhanced Raman scattering SERS dual-mode sensors of Pb(II) in Chinese medicine samples, respectively. The proposed method is more accurate, economical, and simpler to operate than conventional instruments and other single detection modes for detecting heavy metal ions in TCM samples.

Yifan Cui: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Qiulan Li: Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision. Dezhi Yang: Writing – review & editing. Yaling Yang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117) and Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 202M20212118105).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doped carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging applications: A minireview

M. Bottini, T. Mustelin, Carbon materials-Nanosynthesis by candlelight, Nat. Nanotechnology. 2 (2007) 599-600, 10.1038/nnano.2007.316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging metal doped carbon dots for promising theranostic applications
